Soil organic matter — Organic carbon content in the topsoil layer, up to 30cm depth, contained in (decomposed) plant and animal residues, tissues and cells (Nachtergaele et al., 2012). 'Soil organic matter' is an indicator of soil fertility and, thus, ecosystem productivity. Regions with low soil organic carbon need CSA practices that alleviate nutrient depletion such as measures to stop erosion to build up soil carbon through organic fertilizers and integrated practices such as agroforestry (Lal et al., 2011).

Agroforestry interventions significantly reduced climate-related yield losses (Odds Ratio = 0.67). Shaded coffee systems maintain lower temperature stress and enhance soil moisture retention during dry spells.
Agroforestry practices consistently provide benefits, reducing the degradation of coffee bean quality caused by climate stressors (Odds Ratio = 0.74) and microclimate buffering.
Agroforestry enhances the adaptive capacity of farming systems by diversifying production and income, improving soil functions and water regulation, and increasing on-farm biodiversity.
Agroforestry practices regulate hydrology and runoff. Trees intercept rainfall and improve soil infiltration rates, helping mitigate floods.
Agroforestry practices prevent soil erosion on slopes. Roots hold soil particles and improve slope stability.
Our meta-analysis on studies which have been conducted in SSA showed that agroforestry regulates hydrological flow, buffering water runoff.
On a larger geographic scale, agroforestry trees accrue benefits including soil erosion reduction.
This meta-analysis indicates that cocoa agroforestry systems provide ecosystem services such as adaptation to climate change and drought buffering.
Microclimate parameters are buffered in cocoa agroforestry: shade canopy reduces maximum understorey air temperature compared to full sun monocultures.
This may be part of the reason why we found that agroforestry particularly benefits maize yields in strongly water-limited surroundings.
Agroforestry shade trees buffer understorey climate, reducing ambient and crop canopy temperatures.
Canopy shade trees buffer temperature extremes, lowering maximum temperature by up to 2-5 degrees C.
Agroforestry adoption is significantly related to increased household capacity to cope with drought in dryland contexts.
Trees on farm reduce the damage caused by heavy rain and floods, maintaining field structure and preventing crop wash-away.
Alley-cropping strips reduced peak runoff from watersheds by 10-30%, mitigating local flood impact.
Agroforestry vegetative buffer strips significantly improve soil hydraulic conductivity and infiltration rates, reducing runoff.
Windbreaks reduce wind velocity downstream for distances up to 15-20 times the height of the shelterbelt, mitigating wind-erosion.
Alley cropping with vegetative hedges reduces surface runoff by 15-40% compared to monocultures.
Systematic review of tree tolerances: specific species show high adaptation and survival rates under waterlogged soil conditions, assisting sub-drainage.
Wind-tunnel tests confirm that shelterbelts create substantial drag and reduce windward wind speed to mitigate local turbulence.
Agroforestry plays a pivotal role in disaster risk management, specifically buffering the impacts of agricultural drought on livelihoods.
Catchment agroforestry is recommended in regional policies to buffer run-off and manage flash-floods in agricultural landscapes.
World Bank operations upweight agroforestry because of its demonstrated capacity to buffer farmers against drought shocks.
Agroforestry systems serve as key nature-based components of watershed flood-risk management strategies.
Agroforestry practices, particularly silvopastoral and silvoarable, serve as a sustainable land use option to reduce wildfire risk by controlling understorey biomass.
Adoption of agroforestry practices combined with land tenure security leads to a reduction of fire hazard in forest zones.
Silvopasture management reduces fuel loads and mitigates wildfire risk by reducing herbaceous fuel height and mass.
WOCAT database contains global SLM technologies showing that agroforestry practices mitigate drought hazards by improving water infiltration and soil moisture retention.
Water harvesting and soil conservation structures under agroforestry systems reduce surface runoff and peak runoff velocity, mitigating local flooding.
Agroforestry practices recorded in WOCAT stabilize slopes and control soil erosion.
Tenure security is a key driver for the long-term investment required in agroforestry tree establishment, with positive adoption coefficients globally.
Access to credit and financial subsidies significantly increases tree planting and management adoption globally.
Proximity to markets and roads (shorter distance) facilitates sales of agroforestry products and boosts adoption.
Lin (2007) demonstrates that shade trees buffer coffee microclimates, lowering understorey temperatures by up to 2-3 °C during diurnal heat peaks.
Shade canopy alters radiation and keeps relative humidity higher, mitigating potential microclimate extremes and soil water depletion in coffee systems.
Vaast et al. (2006) reported that coffee leaf temperatures under shade trees were up to 4 °C lower than those exposed to full sun, preventing leaf scorching.
Cleugh (1998) shows that windbreaks shelter crops from mechanical wind stress, reducing wind speeds by 20-50% within a downwind distance of 10-15 times tree height.
Sudmeyer et al. (2004) show that windbreaks reduce wind speed and crop lodging, increasing wheat yields in protected zones by 15%.
Damianidis et al. (2020) note that silvopastoral grazing patterns significantly reduce surface grass fuel load, reducing fire intensity and flame height by up to 40%.
Batcheler et al. (2024) show that sheep grazing in silvopasture reduced herbaceous fuel mass from 2.5 t/ha to less than 0.8 t/ha, preventing the ignition of ladder fuels.
Toky et al. (2011) demonstrate that biodrainage using Eucalyptus tereticornis strip plantations lowered the shallow water table by 0.8-1.2 m over 3 years, reclaiming waterlogged soils.
Singh and Lal (2018) review case studies confirming biodrainage can lower water tables by 1-1.5 m in canal-irrigated command areas, reclaiming land and stabilizing crop yields.
Chhabra and Thakur (2005) confirm that biodrainage tree species extract up to 15,000 m3/ha of water annually, mitigating waterlogging in heavy clay soils.
